facts.text: PostgreSQL enforces max_recursive_depth (default 1000) as a hard safety limit to prevent infinite recursion in recursive CTEs from consuming unlimited system resources
PostgreSQL 14+ supports SEARCH DEPTH FIRST/BREADTH FIRST clauses and CYCLE detection for recursive CTEs, enabling safe traversal of cyclic graphs without manual array path tracking
REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW without CONCURRENTLY acquires ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock, blocking all reads and writes. CONCURRENTLY requires a UNIQUE index and is slower but allows concurrent reads
PostgreSQL 12 introduced automatic CTE inlining: non-recursive, side-effect-free, singly-referenced CTEs are inlined by default and treated as subqueries, removing the optimization fence
PostgreSQL 17 improves pathkey hoisting for complex CTEs with ORDER BY clauses, allowing more efficient sorting and merge joins in outer queries
GIN indexes are 3x faster than GiST for text search. Properly optimized GIN with fastupdate=off achieved 50x speedup on 10-million-row dataset (from ~41301ms to ~877ms)
JSONB queries can be 2000x slower than normalized relational schemas for structured, high-volume workloads without proper statistics and indexing
JSONB storage requires 2x the disk space of normalized schemas due to repeated key storage in each document
PostgreSQL's planner cannot keep statistics on values within JSONB columns, leading to severe selectivity mis-estimates and suboptimal query plans
Bridge tables (junction tables) connecting two entities with high cardinality disparity can suffer from explosive row growth and expensive multi-way joins
Full-text search with tsvector decomposes text into lexemes with positions; ts_rank() scoring multiplies base rank by weight factors (A=1.0, B=0.4, C=0.2, D=0.1)
Recursive CTEs with array-based path tracking can detect cycles by checking if a node already appears in the path array accumulated during traversal
Materialized views are not incremental — PostgreSQL recomputes the entire underlying query on every refresh, potentially taking minutes for large result sets
PostgreSQL 17 propagates column statistics from materialized CTEs upward, fixing mis-estimates in selectivity calculations that previously caused suboptimal plans
Type 2 Slowly Changing Dimensions use surrogate keys (not source system primary keys) with effective-date ranges (FromDate/ToDate) to track historical versions of dimension members
WITHOUT CONCURRENTLY requires no index; WITH CONCURRENTLY requires a UNIQUE index (no expressions, no WHERE clauses, must include all rows)
PostgreSQL evaluates CTEs only to the extent that rows are actually fetched by the parent query, so LIMIT in the parent can prevent infinite recursion during testing
Materialized view indexes are the highest-leverage tuning step after materialization — without them, every read scans the entire table, eroding the speed advantage
to_tsvector performs stemming/lemmatization to reduce tokens to lexeme form and removes stop words (the, and, etc.); setweight assigns A-D labels for title/body/section weighting

examples.description: Using recursive CTEs to traverse bill-of-materials (BOM) structures where a finished product contains assemblies which contain sub-assemblies; recursive CTE expands the full BOM tree to calculate total cost and inventory requirements

Recursive CTEs for traversing organizational hierarchies to find all subordinates under a manager, calculate reporting depth, or identify sibling teams; SEARCH BREADTH FIRST ensures level-order traversal
Recursive CTEs to discover hidden friendship paths between users; CYCLE clause prevents revisiting already-traversed friends and blocks infinite loops in undirected friend graphs
Recursive CTEs to traverse directory hierarchies, finding all files under a path by joining on parent_id; depth counter prevents runaway recursion in deep directory trees

Bridge tables connecting fact tables to multiple slowly-changing dimensions; performance degrades with high cardinality disparity (e.g., 1M products × 10K suppliers) due to cartesian-product-like row explosion in the bridge

Materialized views for slow aggregation queries (e.g., user-per-cohort counts, revenue by region); refresh on pg_cron schedule every 15min; GIN indexes on filtered dimensions for sub-second drill-downs
Full-text search with tsvector indexed by GIN; ts_rank() scores results by query relevance; setweight boosts title matches (weight A) over body matches (weight D)
Query on 100M-row table with complex CTE containing ORDER BY clause now benefits from pathkey hoisting, reducing unnecessary re-sorting in outer query
JSONB used for semi-structured customer attributes; without proper GIN indexing and column statistics awareness, simple filter queries suffered 2000x slowdown vs. normalized schema
Type 2 SCD tracking customer address changes over time; surrogate key + FromDate/ToDate allows fact table to reference exact address version valid on transaction date

analogies.analogy: A recursive CTE is like a breadth-first search queue implemented in pure SQL: the anchor is the initial queue, the recursive term dequeues and enqueues neighbors, and UNION ALL accumulates all visited nodes. No need to loop in application code.

analogies.concept: CTE optimization fence vs query barrier
analogies.analogy: Pre-PostgreSQL 12, a CTE was like a brick wall the query planner couldn't see through: it would materialize the entire CTE result into a temporary table before applying filters from the outer query. Modern inlining punches holes in the wall, letting the planner see through and push predicates inside.

analogies.concept: Materialized view staleness vs cached data
analogies.analogy: A materialized view is like a snapshot photo on a wall — fast to look at, but it was taken 15 minutes ago. If the real scene (source data) changes now, the wall photo doesn't update until you manually take a new photo (REFRESH).

JSONB is like storing a filing cabinet (all folders and files) as a single photograph. Normalized tables are like organizing the same cabinet into labeled drawers. To find one file in the photograph, you search the entire image; in labeled drawers, you go straight to the right one.

A bridge table connecting products to suppliers is like a cross-reference index in a book. If you have 1M products and 10K suppliers, the index can theoretically have 10 billion entries if every product is sold by every supplier. Real data rarely hits that extreme, but many-to-many relationships create risk of explosive growth.

tsvector is like indexing the page numbers where each word appears in a book. ts_rank() is like a relevance score: a book where the query words are dense on a few pages (high rank) beats one where they're scattered. setweight is like putting bookmarks on chapter titles (weight A) vs footnotes (weight D).

Type 2 SCD is like a medical record with patient history: instead of overwriting the old address, you add a new record and mark the old one as 'effective through 2024-12-31'. Time-travel queries see the address valid on any date.
Turning off fastupdate is like batching mail instead of checking it 100 times a day: slower writes to update the index, but much faster reads because the index is always optimized.

key_numbers.context: Hard safety limit for recursive CTEs; prevents infinite loops from consuming unbounded resources

key_numbers.metric: GIN index speedup over unoptimized FTS
key_numbers.context: Benchmark on 10-million-row dataset with proper GIN indexing and fastupdate=off

key_numbers.metric: GIN vs GiST lookup speed ratio
key_numbers.context: GIN indexes are preferred for text search; one lookup in GIN is ~3x faster than GiST
Documented case where unoptimized JSONB queries on structured data without proper indexing/statistics were 2000x slower than normalized relational schema
Normalized schema uses ~50% less disk than JSONB due to repeated key storage in each document
Default weights applied to lexeme positions for setweight-based relevance scoring
CTEs matching these criteria are automatically inlined and treated as subqueries, removing optimization fence
Blocks all reads and writes until refresh completes; can take minutes on large views

key_numbers.metric: Concurrent materialized view refresh index requirement
key_numbers.value: UNIQUE constraint (no expressions, no WHERE clauses)
key_numbers.context: Required to enable CONCURRENTLY option for non-blocking refreshes
Native cycle detection for recursive CTEs without manual array path tracking

common_misconceptions.misconception: Recursive CTEs are always slow because they're materialized
common_misconceptions.truth: PostgreSQL 12+ inlines singly-referenced, non-recursive CTEs by default, removing the optimization fence. Only CTEs referenced multiple times are materialized. Developers can control inlining with MATERIALIZED/NOT MATERIALIZED keywords.

common_misconceptions.misconception: JSONB is always faster than normalized tables because it avoids joins
common_misconceptions.truth: JSONB is 2000x slower in worst case (unstructured, no indexing, high selectivity). Normalized schemas with proper indexes consistently outperform JSONB for structured, high-volume workloads. JSONB shines only when schema flexibility genuinely demands it.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Materialized views are automatically refreshed and always up-to-date
common_misconceptions.truth: PostgreSQL has no built-in auto-refresh. Views are stale until REFRESH runs, which takes an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock (blocking all reads) unless CONCURRENTLY is used (which is slower). Refresh must be manually scheduled via pg_cron or external jobs.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Full-text search in PostgreSQL is slow and requires Elasticsearch
common_misconceptions.truth: PostgreSQL full-text search with GIN indexes achieves sub-second query times on millions of documents. Benchmark: properly optimized GIN achieved 50x speedup (41s → 877ms). For small-to-medium apps, Postgres FTS often outperforms the complexity and cost of Elasticsearch.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Recursive CTEs can run infinitely without safeguards
common_misconceptions.truth: PostgreSQL enforces max_recursive_depth (default 1000) as a hard limit. Queries exceeding this depth error immediately. The setting can be lowered per session for stricter early warnings.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Bridge tables connecting many-to-many relationships have predictable size
common_misconceptions.truth: Bridge table size explodes with cardinality disparity. A bridge connecting 1M products to 10K suppliers can theoretically have 10B rows. Many-to-many performance degrades nonlinearly as cardinality grows.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Type 1 SCD (overwrite) is sufficient for tracking dimensional changes
common_misconceptions.truth: Type 1 SCD loses historical data. Type 2 SCD maintains full audit trail with surrogate keys and effective-date ranges, enabling time-travel queries and regulatory compliance. Choice depends on historical reporting requirements.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Materialized view indexes are optional, just for minor speedups
common_misconceptions.truth: Indexes on materialized views are the highest-leverage tuning step. Without them, every read scans the entire materialized table, completely eroding the performance benefit of materialization.

common_misconceptions.misconception: CTE MATERIALIZED forces a performance cliff; you should never use it
common_misconceptions.truth: MATERIALIZED is critical for CTEs referenced multiple times. Computing the CTE once and reusing the result is far faster than re-computing it for each reference. Use MATERIALIZED intentionally when the CTE is called 2+ times.

common_misconceptions.misconception: PostgreSQL 12+ CTE improvements eliminated all optimization concerns
common_misconceptions.truth: While inlining helps singly-referenced CTEs, materialized CTEs (pre-computed temporary results) and multi-referenced CTEs still carry performance trade-offs. PostgreSQL 17 improves statistics propagation, but query plan analysis via EXPLAIN ANALYZE is essential to verify performance.
